name: judge
description: Scoring framework for test-kitchen cookoff and omakase-off. Invoked at Phase 4 to evaluate implementations using 5-criteria scoring. Do not invoke directly - called by cookoff/omakase-off.
capabilities: Scores implementations on 5 criteria: fitness, complexity, readability, robustness, maintainability
Fills out detailed scoring worksheets with checklist items
Applies hard gates: fitness delta >= 2 triggers auto-win, critical flaws eliminate impls
Produces winner selection with rationale and trade-off analysis
Works for both cookoff (same design, different implementations) and omakase (different approaches)
author: 2389-research
securitySummary: This skill is a pure markdown documentation file containing only scoring instructions. No executable code exists in the skill. All 24 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the static scanner misidentified plain text in JSON metadata and markdown documentation as security vulnerabilities. There is no network access, file system operations, or command execution capability. The skill only provides structured prompts for AI-assisted code evaluation.
valueStatement: When multiple implementations compete in cookoff or omakase-off, the judge skill evaluates each using 5 criteria to pick the winner. This ensures quality decisions when parallel agents produce different results.
userTitle: Score and compare parallel code implementations
limitations: Not invoked directly - only called by cookoff or omakase-off skills
Requires implementations to already exist in context
Does not run tests or execute code - only evaluates code visible in conversation
Integer scores only - no half points or nuanced scoring

useCases.targetUser: Development teams using parallel implementation workflows
useCases.title: Cookoff evaluation
useCases.description: Score competing implementations of the same design to pick the best approach

useCases.title: Omakase-off comparison
useCases.description: Evaluate different architectural approaches and select the optimal solution

useCases.title: Automated quality gates
useCases.description: Apply consistent scoring criteria across multiple implementations

promptTemplates.title: Basic scoring request
promptTemplates.scenario: When implementations are ready for evaluation
promptTemplates.prompt: Judge the implementations in context using the 5-criteria scoring framework. Fill out complete worksheets for each impl.

promptTemplates.title: Cookoff evaluation
promptTemplates.prompt: Run the judge skill on impl-1, impl-2, and impl-3 from the cookoff. Apply all scoring criteria and announce the winner.

promptTemplates.title: Omakase comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare variant-a and variant-b using the judge skill. Note that these are different approaches, not different implementations of the same design.

promptTemplates.title: Detailed analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Provide complete scoring worksheets with all checklist items filled. Include feasibility checks and hard gate results. Explain trade-offs between all implementations.

bestPractices: Ensure all implementations are visible in context before invoking judge
Run fresh-eyes review on survivors before judge evaluation
Document the selection rationale for future reference
antiPatterns: Invoking judge directly - it should be called by cookoff or omakase-off
Skipping feasibility checks - they catch critical issues early
Using half-point scores - only integer scores 1-5 are valid

faq.question: How is the winner determined?
faq.answer: Highest scoring implementation wins unless a hard gate triggers. Fitness gate auto-advances higher fitness impl. Critical flaws eliminate.

faq.question: Can I invoke judge directly?
faq.answer: No. Judge is called automatically by cookoff or omakase-off at Phase 4. Direct invocation may produce incomplete results.

faq.question: What happens if implementations have equal scores?
faq.answer: The skill does not specify tie-breaking. Review the worksheets to identify differences or request human judgment.

faq.question: Does judge run tests?
faq.answer: No. Judge assumes tests already ran and results are available in context. It evaluates code and design, not test execution.

faq.question: Can judge evaluate more than 3 implementations?
faq.answer: The format shows 3 columns but can be extended. The skill works for any number of implementations with columns added.

faq.question: What is the difference between cookoff and omakase evaluation?
faq.answer: Cookoff compares implementations of same design. Omakase compares different architectural approaches. Scoring is same but interpretation differs.
